summary: Jeff Atwood examines the rapid adoption of .NET in 2004, citing Forrester Research data showing 56% of developers chose .NET as their primary platform versus 44% for J2EE. He attributes this success to three factors: .NET's heritage from Delphi through Anders Hejlsberg, Visual Studio's superior productivity, and the massive existing Visual Basic developer base. Atwood argues that Microsoft's timing was critical, as Java was threatening to dominate enterprise development. He concludes that Microsoft earned this mindshare legitimately by producing a quality product.
key_points: .NET adoption surged past J2EE in 2004, with developers citing ease of use and faster time to market as primary reasons
.NET is essentially 'Delphi Reloaded' thanks to Microsoft hiring Anders Hejlsberg from Borland
Visual Studio .NET set the gold standard for IDE productivity, which directly drove adoption
Microsoft's timing was crucial—a few years later and Java might have permanently captured enterprise developers
Developer mindshare is the key strategic asset, and Microsoft earned it through product quality rather than just marketing
money_quotes: .NET is basically Delphi Reloaded (that's what you get when you hire Anders Hejlsberg away from Borland). It's even better!
The Visual Studio .NET IDE is the gold standard in developer productivity. I'm not aware of any other IDE that even comes close.
Yes, the most usable product is the one people end up using. Go figure.
Where Microsoft goes, so go the Visual Basic developers – the largest group of developers in the world.
Own that mindshare, and you own the world.
The great thing about .NET is that, whatever your opinion of company, Microsoft is gaining mindshare the old fashioned way: by producing a quality product.
key_insight: Developer tools win market share through genuine usability and productivity gains, not marketing—the most usable product is simply the one people end up using.
